one_line_takeaway: Machine-unlearning methods that pass standard logit/MIA evaluations often leave the forgotten data's influence intact in the encoder; this paper's representation-based, ImageNet-scale benchmark (CKA + k-NN, AGR/H-LR, plus a semantic Top Class-wise Forgetting scenario) exposes that false sense of security.
key_finding: Logit-based success does not imply representation-level forgetting. Every unlearned baseline is more similar (CKA) to the original model theta_o than to the retrained oracle theta_r (the 'CKA(theta_u,theta_r) > CKA(theta_u,theta_o)' column is a cross for all nine methods, tick only for theta_r). Methods split into two failure modes: some (GA, RL, SalUn) collapse the encoder representation entirely (AGR ~0.04-0.06), while others (DUCK, CU, SCAR, SCRUB) merely tweak the classifier head, keeping high logit scores while the encoder stays close to theta_o. The authors attribute this to 'shortcut learning' — algorithms modify mainly the last layer to satisfy logit losses. Strikingly, Pseudo Labeling (PL), which does NOT use the retain set, is the strongest approximate method overall (AGL 0.94, AGR 0.84, H-LR 0.89), essentially matching DUCK (H-LR 0.90) and beating retain-set methods CU/SCAR/SCRUB — reversing the ranking seen in small-scale benchmarks. Top Class-wise Forgetting further separates methods (e.g., CU's AGR drops sharply) that look fine under random forgetting.
core_contribution: This is a measurement/benchmark paper, not a new unlearning algorithm. Its own contribution is a critical re-examination of how machine unlearning is evaluated. The authors argue that the field's standard logit-based metrics (Forget/Retain Accuracy, MIA) on small-scale tasks (e.g., forgetting one class in CIFAR-10) give a 'false sense of security,' and they propose a unified, representation-aware evaluation framework. Concretely they contribute: (1) representation-based metrics built on Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA, similarity of encoder features between the unlearned model theta_u and the retrained oracle theta_r) and k-NN transfer accuracy on downstream datasets, aggregated into an AGR (Average GAP of Representation-based metrics) score and combined with the logit-based AGL score into a single harmonic measure H-LR; (2) a large-scale evaluation protocol on ImageNet-1K (1.28M images, forgetting 100-300 classes) using ResNet-50, Swin-T, and ConvNeXt, versus prior work capped at 1-10 forget classes; and (3) 'Top Class-wise Forgetting,' a new scenario that deliberately selects forget classes semantically similar to downstream-task classes so that residual representational knowledge cannot hide behind alternative retained classes. Their empirical re-evaluation of existing methods is the finding, not the method itself.
threat_model: Standard machine-unlearning privacy setting (GDPR-style 'right to be forgotten'): a model theta_o trained on a full dataset D must have the influence of a forget set D_f removed to produce theta_u, ideally indistinguishable from a model theta_r retrained only on the retain set D_r. The security-relevant claim is that current evaluations UNDER-estimate residual information: methods that pass logit-based/MIA checks can still retain the forgotten data's influence at the representation (encoder) level, so a party auditing an unlearned encoder (or reusing it for downstream transfer) could recover the supposedly-erased information. The paper is defensive/measurement in nature; it proposes no new attack, but shows that existing unlearning may only mask forgetting rather than achieve it.
limitations: Scope is image classification with CNN/transformer encoders only; no NLP/generative-model unlearning is evaluated despite privacy being invoked. The framework relies on training a full retrained oracle theta_r as ground truth, which is exactly the expensive step approximate unlearning tries to avoid — so the benchmark is a research tool, not a deployable audit. CKA and k-NN measure representational similarity/transferability, not a formal privacy guarantee or a concrete extraction attack, so 'residual information' is inferred rather than exploited. The Top Class-wise selection depends on a cosine-similarity ranking of class-averaged features whose sensitivity is not deeply ablated, and results are reported for a fixed set of downstream datasets (Office-Home, CUB, DomainNet-126).

datasets_used: ImageNet-1K (1.28M images; unlearning source, 100-300 forget classes)
CUB-200-2011 (200 bird species, 11,788 images; downstream transfer)
CIFAR-10 (used to contrast small-scale vs large-scale AGL)
quantitative_results: Logit-based AGL on ImageNet-1K (higher=better, oracle=1.00): PL 0.94, DUCK 0.96, CU 0.92, SCAR 0.87, SCRUB 0.80, FT 0.78, SalUn 0.15, GA 0.12, RL 0.12
Representation AGR on ImageNet-1K: DUCK 0.85, PL 0.84, FT 0.81, CU 0.78, SCAR 0.64, SCRUB 0.60, GA 0.06, SalUn 0.06, RL 0.04
Unified H-LR: DUCK 0.90, PL 0.89, CU 0.84, FT 0.79, SCAR 0.74, SCRUB 0.69, SalUn 0.09, GA 0.08, RL 0.06 (retrained oracle 1.00)
PL (no retain set) matches/beats retain-set methods, reversing small-scale rankings: PL H-LR 0.89 vs CU 0.84 / SCAR 0.74 / SCRUB 0.69
All nine unlearned baselines are more CKA-similar to theta_o than to theta_r (CKA(theta_u,theta_r) > CKA(theta_u,theta_o) column = cross for every method)
Original model FA 79.2 / RA 80.1 / TFA 76.1 / TRA 76.5; retrained oracle drives FA and TFA to 0.0 with RA 76.0
CKA(theta_o, theta_r) is ~88% under Random Class-wise Forgetting and drops under Top Class-wise Forgetting, making unlearning verification more stringent
RL and SalUn achieve high AGL on CIFAR-10 (~0.89 and ~0.99) but degrade to ~0.39 on ImageNet-1K
